text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Solutions and Strategies

Have students discuss the rate tables.

Suggested Questions

If students do not explicitly mention the role of the unit rate, push them to think about it with these questions. (Language)

  • Which tables were easier for you to complete, the tables in Problem 3.1 or these?
  • How can the unit rate help you find equivalent ratios in the table?

• Most likely students will think that the patterns in this problem were easier. Students should mention using multiples of 4 or adding 4 more cups for each ounce of popcorn kernels.
  • If you know how much it is for 1 unit of something, it is easy to multiply by the greater quantity to find the price or amount

Ask students to explain the advantages of the rate table.

  • How can the rate table help you answer questions about popping popcorn and making servings?
  • how can you answer questions about the popcorn when the numbers are not on the table? For example, how do you find the number of cups of popcorn you can make from 30 ounces of popcorn kernels?

• Answers will vary.
  • Some students may extend the table. Some students may imagine making jumps on the table, like multiplying the amount for 10 ounces of kernels by 3. Some students will use a general expression, like for every 1 ounce of kernels, you get 4 cups of popcorn.

With each strategy that students discuss, have them justify why the strategy work. Then ask students to connect how the different strategy works. Then ask students to connect how the different strategies represent the same type of thinking.

  • How do you find how many ounces of popcorn kernels are needed to make 100 cups of popcorn?
  • How do the rte tables help you find unit rates?
  • How do the rate tables help you write algebraic expressions for the relationship between the variables?

• Some students may extend the table. Some students may imagine making jumps on the table, like multiplying the amount for 20 cups of popcorn by 5 to get 100 cups of popcorn. Some students will use a general expression, like the number of cups of popcorn divided by 4 tells how many ounces of kernels are needed.
  • Use the ratio reasoning in the tables to get oe of the quantities equal to 1.
  • The tables help you to see the repeated pattern that can be written as a rule.

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Suggested Questions

  • How are unit rates helpful?
  • How can I write a comparison statement using a unit rate?

• They help find any equivalent ratio; they help you write an expression or equation.
  • A unit rate is a comparison where one of the quantities is 1, so a comparison statement something like: For every 1 (something), there is (some amount of another quantity).

There is a classroom conversation about writing the equation in What If...? Situation B:

    Teacher: how do you describe the relationship between the number of servings S you get from K ounces of kernels?

    Wren: We noticed that when you know the number of cups of popcorn, you just divide that by two.

    Teacher: Can you tell me more about that?

    Wren: 4 cups of popcorn is 2 servings because a serving is 2 cups. In the Initial Challenge, we found that 12 ounces of kernels made 48 cups of popcorn, so divide that by 2 and you would get 24 servings.

    Teacher: What do others think about Wren's group idea?

    Matthew: That works on all of them in the table. (He shows his table and points out that it works for 5 ounces, 8 ounces, and 10 ounces)

Teacher: How can we use the description of this relationship to write an equation?

    Scarlett: Using what Wren said, "The number of cups of popcorn divided by 2 gets the number of servings," we wrote this:

    S = 4k/2

    Teacher: Can you tell me more about how your equation represents the relationship that Wren was describing?

    Scarlett: Sure. First you have to find the number of cups of popcorn. We knew from the beginning of the problem that this was found by taking 4 times the number of kernels (that is our 4k). Then when you have that, you divide that number by 2 to get the servings, S. So servings equals 4 times the kernels divided by 2, which is what S = 4k/2 means.

    Teacher: What do the others think about this idea?

    Jordan: I don't know. It makes sense when Scarlett is describing it, but we got a different equation, and I think ours is right. We wrote S = 2K. If you look at the table, it works for any of the places on the table. If you multiply the number of ounces of kernels by 2, you get the number of servings. (He demonstrates with 3 ounces, 5 ounces, and 12 ounces as examples.)

Teacher: What do others think about this idea?

    Rachael: I think they are both correct. They are looking at different things about the situation. Scarlett was thinking about it from finding the number of cups of popcorn first, and Jordan was thinking about it from starting with the number of ounces of popcorn kernels.

    Ashley: Yeah. And look at the two equations. If you take Scarlett's equation of S = 4k/2 and you divide 4 by 2, you end up with Jordan's equation S = 2K.

    Jordan: You do! Scarlett just showed how to get the popcorn first and then the number of servings. I just used the kernels and went right to the number of servings. I think they are equivalent; you could use either one.

    (Many heads nod in the classroom in agreement.)

  • In What If...? Situation C, how did you find the cost of 12 bags of unpopped corn?
• Answers will vary. Some students may multiply 3.5 by 12; some may create a rate table; some may make a graph.
• In What If...? Situation C, how did you find the number of bags of unpopped corn for $15?

• Answers will vary. Some students may take $15 can divide it by 3.5, and since it is 4.28..., they will say the most you can get is 4 bags; some students may use a rate table and say that it is between 4 and 5 bags so the most you can get is 4 bags; some students may use a graph and say that it is between 4 and 5 bags so the most you can get is 4 bags.

Note that vocabulary of expression and equation was introduced in Variables and Patterns. You may have to help emphasize the difference between an expression and an equation. An expression uses variables and/or numbers to make a mathematical statement. (For example, 4K represents 4 times the number of ounces of kernels.) Equations show equivalence between expressions. (For example, 4K = C represents 4 times the number of ounces of kernels equals the number of cups of popcorn.)
